coredata.dc:title: Upcycling of plastic waste into fluorescent carbon dots: An environmentally viable transformation to biocompatible C-dots with potential prospective in analytical applications
coredata.dc:creator: Chaudhary, Savita
Kumari, Manisha
Chauhan, Pooja
Ram Chaudhary, Ganga
coredata.dc:description: The profitable impact on ecological system made the upcycling of plastic waste as one of the captivating issues in scientific world. The current work highlights the sustainable approach to transform the plastic waste comprises of bottles, used cups and polyethylene bags via simple heating to fluorescent carbon dots (C-dots). The obtained C-dots have displayed the absorption peaks around at 260 nm with size ranging between 5 and 30 nm. The upcycling has produced the structural changes in plastic waste and affected the optical properties of C-dots. The three types of used plastic waste as precursor have displayed excellent emission properties with peak positioned around 422 nm and quantum yield (QY) values ∼62, 65 and 64% for C-dots generated from plastic polybags, cups and bottles (P-CDs, C-CDs and B-CDs) respectively. The toxicity profiling of C-dots has been successfully tested by employing multi-assay biocompatible activities i.e. antibacterial and antifungal activities. The potential prospective of C-dots derived from plastic waste have further been explored in analytical applications involving selective copper metal ion sensing in aqueous media. The outcomes of the current studies have highlighted the potential accomplishment in preserving environment fate and giving response towards the budding social hitch of plastic waste.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Plastic waste
Carbon dots
Upcycling
Quantum yield
Toxicity
Metal ion sensing

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure body mmlmath acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Analytical sensing performance of C-dots in presence of metal ions
Analytical sensing performance of C-dots in real water samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Haryana State Council for Science and Technology

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) FTIR survey scan, (b) XRD profile, and (c–e) HRTEM micrographs of P-CDs, C-CDs and B-CDs.
(a) TGA and (b) DSC curves of P-CDs, C-CDs and B-CDs.
Excitation wavelength dependent fluorescence emission profile of (a) P-CDs, (b) C-CDs and (c) B-CDs. (d) Variation of Quantum yield value as a function of wavelength for P-CDs, C-CDs and B-CDs.
(a) Antimicrobial activities (b) fungus growth and (b) antifungal index values (%) as a function of concentrations for P-CDs, C-CDs and B-CDs.
Pictorial representation of different stages in the root tips of Allium cepa in presence of P-CDs.

Fluorescence emission profile of (a) P-CDs, (b) C-CDs (c) B-CDs in presence of different metal ions. The fluorescence-based titration effect of different concentration of Cu2+ ions for (d) P-CDs, (e) C-CDs (e) B-CDs.
Fluorescence responses of the (a) P-CDs, (b) C-CDs (c) B-CDs for different common cations in presence Cu2+ ions.
Recovery study of (a) P-CDs, (b) C-CDs (c) B-CDs in presence of real water samples.
Schematic illustration showing the formation of C-dots from different types of plastic waste.
Schematic illustration showing the mechanistic behavior of C-dots towards copper metal ion.

head.ce:title: Upcycling of plastic waste into fluorescent carbon dots: An environmentally viable transformation to biocompatible C-dots with potential prospective in analytical applications

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and Centre of Advanced Studies in Chemistry, Panjab University, Chandigarh 160014, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and Centre of Advanced Studies in Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry and Centre of Advanced Studies in Chemistry, Panjab University, Chandigarh 160014, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Conversion of mixed plastic waste into biocompatible, Antioxidative and fluorescent carbon dots.

The profitable impact on ecological system made the upcycling of plastic waste as one of the captivating issues in scientific world. The current work highlights the sustainable approach to transform the plastic waste comprises of bottles, used cups and polyethylene bags via simple heating to fluorescent carbon dots (C-dots). The obtained C-dots have displayed the absorption peaks around at 260 nm with size ranging between 5 and 30 nm. The upcycling has produced the structural changes in plastic waste and affected the optical properties of C-dots. The three types of used plastic waste as precursor have displayed excellent emission properties with peak positioned around 422 nm and quantum yield (QY) values ∼62, 65 and 64% for C-dots generated from plastic polybags, cups and bottles (P-CDs, C-CDs and B-CDs) respectively. The toxicity profiling of C-dots has been successfully tested by employing multi-assay biocompatible activities i.e. antibacterial and antifungal activities. The potential prospective of C-dots derived from plastic waste have further been explored in analytical applications involving selective copper metal ion sensing in aqueous media. The outcomes of the current studies have highlighted the potential accomplishment in preserving environment fate and giving response towards the budding social hitch of plastic waste.

ce:section.ce:para: The low production cost, lightweight, high strength with long durability has popularized the usage of plastic in every sphere of life by replacing various commercially used materials (Alam et al., 2018; Veerakumar et al., 2018). The different form of plastic in automobiles, food packaging industries, educational institutions, construction, biomedical equipments, pharmaceutics have estimated around 6.5–8.5 million ton/year usage of plastic in India itself (Aryan et al., 2019). The excessive usage of low priced non-biodegradable plastic sources is considered as one of the major contributors of waste that further directed to major environmental and health concerns (Chen et al., 2020). In addition, plastics are being one of the major constituents in discarded electronic wastes and adversely affected the environment (Hu et al., 2019). On the other hand, the low rate of recyclability has made the single use plastics including plastic bags, packaging material, cups, and bottles as one of the major contributors of dumping landfills (Ronkay et al., 2021). The improper management of plastic waste on landfills has produced the leaching of chemicals from plastics and causes hazardous environmental pollution of soil, air pollution and underground water pollution. The consumption of polluted water has further affected the respiratory functioning of living beings and caused cancers in extreme cases. Therefore, keeping the seriousness of the problem in mind, researcher should do efforts towards the annihilation of plastic problem via upcycling the plastic waste to useful advanced materials (Welle, 2011). In general, these plastic wastes are utilized to construct roads, or transformed to provide fuel or energy or reprocessed to use in construction material (Coelho et al., 2011; Berkmans et al., 2014; Barnes et al., 2009; Hopewell et al., 2009; Cleetus et al., 2013). In recent era, various reports have been published for the development of plastic waste derived C-dots (Table S1, supplementary data). For instance, Ramanan et al. (2018) have used an alternative means to transform generated plastic waste via using polyethylene terephthalate to recycled polyester fibers (Ramanan et al., 2018). Huang et al. (2014) have transformed the waste plastic materials to luminescent C-dots (Huang et al., 2014). However, the developed methodology was found to be quite tedious with multistep processing by using toxic chemical treatment. The major concern of these methodologies was the involvement of high temperature based hydrothermal processing in presence of organic solvent, which further restricted their biological application, such as analytical bio-sensors, tissue engineering and bioimaging devices. Therefore, an optimization of a greener methodology via converting plastic waste to highly luminescent C-dots in aqueous media is urgently required.

In this view, the current work highlights the sustainable approach to transform the plastic wastes comprises of used bottles, cups and polyethylene bags via simple heating into fluorescence carbon dots (C-dots). With the aim of reducing the production cost, saving the natural resources and reinforcing the environment by converting plastic waste into highly luminescent C-dots is the central theme of the work. These plastic wastes have provided a potential feeding stock for the fabrication of economically viable nanomaterials of carbon. The C-dots generated from plastic waste have earned high curiosity because of their superior biocompatibility, adjustable optical and luminescence properties (Zhang et al., 2013). The low-cost synthetic fabrication with ease of scale up has further enhanced their widespread interest in technological applications including sensing, bio-imaging, water treatment and many more (Pramanik et al., 2018). The discharge of large range of industrial effluents including toxic metal ions (50 g/l), textile dyes (7 × 105 ton/year worldwide), and pharmaceutical waste (out of total more than 30% compounds) has pernicious impact over the living beings. The optimized fluorescence properties of C-dots prepared via upcycling of plastic waste has enhanced the practical utilities of prepared nanoparticles in metal ion sensing. The formed C-dots are found to be selective for copper metal ions (Cu2+ ion) in aqueous media and useful in treating metal ion related toxicity issues.

Copper (Cu) is the third most abundant transition element in human being and serve an essential role in biological systems (Wang et al., 2017). It acts as a vital component for the various kinds of enzymes, such as Cu/Zn superoxide dismutase, tyrosinase, cytochrome c oxidase. Copper mainly exist in natural and environmental water (tap water, lake water, seawater, river water and drinking water). Cu is found in human body up to the level of 1.4–2.1 mg/kg of body weight. According to the United States Environmental Protection Agency (USEPA), the safe level of Cu in drinking water is 1.3 ppm (20 µm) (Das et al., 2017). Nevertheless, when Cu is present in excess, its toxicity can create harmful effect on human body. For instance, exposure to high concentration of Cu2+ can produce toxic impact over liver and kidneys (Wang et al., 2020). The unbalanced concentration of Cu2+ in cells has caused various neurodegenerative ailments such as Parkinson disease, Wilson’s disease and even cancer in extreme cases (Chaudhary et al., 2020). For this reason, it is quite essential to sense Cu2+ ions both in the living system and in our environmental surroundings. The traditional investigative methodologies such as atomic absorption spectroscopy, electrochemical sensors, UV–visible method and inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry can satisfy the need to detect Cu2+ ions (Dobrowolska et al., 2008; Jones et al., 1973; Gupta et al., 2006). But their utilization is restrained due to costly instrumentation, complex sample preparation, and complicated experimental processing. Apart from other methodologies, fluorescence-based sensing offers a simple, low-cost, rapid and quantitative means for detecting Cu2+ ions in aqueous media (Bhatt et al., 2018). However, there are limited reports are available in literature towards fluorometric sensing of Cu2+ ions using C-dots prepared from plastic waste. Therefore, it is quite challenging to fabricate an effective fluorescent based probe for selective and sensitive detection of Cu2+ ions in aqueous media, which further enhanced the scope of current work. The sensing aptitude of developed particles is tested in various water samples taken from different natural sources. The low-cost synthetic fabrication strategy with ease of scale up has enhanced their wide spread interest in technological applications and rationalized the advanced nanoparticles as alternative greener sensory probe for Cu2+ ions in aqueous media. The outcomes of the current studies have highlighted the potential accomplishment in preserving environment fate and giving response towards the budding social hitch of plastic waste.

The waste plastic polybags, cups and bottles were collected from the waste bin of Department of Chemistry, Panjab University, Chandigarh, India. Deionized water was utilized as a dispersing solvent for the all sample preparation. Sulphuric acid (H2SO4) and ethanol were procured from Fisher Scientific with 99% purity. Muller Hinton agar and Nutrient broth were purchased from Himedia with 99% purity for antimicrobial activities. Malt extract and Agar-Agar type-I with 99% purity were taken from Himedia for antifungal activities. Quinine sulfate dye was procured from Sigma-Aldrich with 99% purity. The nitrate salts of different metal cations such as Ni2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Fe2+, K+, Ca2+, Hg2+, Na+, Pb2+, Co2+, Mg2+ and Ba2+ were purchased from Sigma- Aldrich with 99% purity. All the chemicals were used as such without doing any further processing.

The fabrication of C-dots was designed with the aim of eco-friendly conversion of single use plastic waste to fluorescent C-dots via one step synthetic approach (Scheme 1 ). The desired C-dots were obtained by optimizing the synthetic conditions in which reaction temperature is a crucial parameter. For the synthesis, plastic wastes such as, plastic bags, plastic cups and plastic bottles were put up in a ceramic crucible and thermal calcinations was performed in a muffle furnace at a moderate temperature of 400 °C for a time period of 2 h under inert atmosphere by purging nitrogen gas through external cylinder. The black-colored obtained powders were cooled down to room temperature and mechanically crushed down to form powdered product. Then, 1gm of formed powder was transferred to 200 ml of distilled water under stirring to produce a carbonized solution of C-dots. The prepared C-dots dispersion was centrifuged at 7000 rpm for 15 min to remove the bigger sized residues. The purification of C-dots was performed by filtering the supernatant solution through a 0.22 µm filter membrane. The ultracentrifugation was done at 17,000 rpm to convert the dispersed C-dots into solid extract of P-CDs, C-CDs and B-CDs (C-dots obtained from waste poly bags, cups and bottles respectively). A reference standard method (equation (1)) was employed for calculating the quantum yield (Φ) of P-CDs, C-CDs and B-CDs. The standard solution of quinine sulfate (54% quantum yield at excitation wavelength of 310 nm) in 0.1 M H2SO4 solution was chosen as reference dye (Gao et al., 2019).
(1)
Φ X
=
Φ R
I x
I R
A R
A X
η X 2
η R 2

Herein, Φ refers to the quantum yield, I correspond to the fluorescence intensity, A denotes to the absorbance intensity in UV–visible spectra, η represents the refractive index. The subscripts R and x refers to the standard and sample respectively.

High Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopic (HRTEM, Hitachi H7500 electron microscope) analysis was executed to measure the size and morphology of the P-CDs, C-CDs and B-CDs at 100 kV. XRD analysis was carried out to evaluate the crystalline size and phases of the as synthesized C-dots utilizing scan rate of 2 deg min−1 in XRD powder diffractometer (Panalytical X’Pert Pro) with Cu-Kα radiation. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) instrument from Malvern (ZEN 1690) was employed to analyze the particle size and polydispersity index of C-dots. Fourier transform infrared spectrum was obtained in the KBr medium between wave number range of 4000–400 cm−1 on Perkin Elmer (RX1) FTIR spectrometer instrument. Thermogravimetric analysis was carried out to investigate the thermal behavior of P-CDs, C-CDs and B-CDs on SDT Q-600 instrument. Ultraviolet–visible absorption spectra were collected from LABINDIA UV–visible spectrophotometer in the wavelength range of 200–800 nm. DSC measurements (Mettler Toledo (Q20)) were performed to scrutinize the enthalpy of transition and phase transition temperatures of P-CDs, C-CDs and B-CDs. Remi R-24 centrifuge was used for carrying out the ultracentrifugation of dispersed solution of the C-dots obtained from the calcination of plastic wastes. Fluorescence emission profile of P-CDs, C-CDs and B-CDs were acquired from a Perkin Elmer LS 55 PL spectrophotometer. The elemental composition of P-CDs, C-CDs and B-CDs were recorded on EDX (JEOL JSM 6100) instrument. The elemental composition of C, H, and N were done on Perkin Elmer 2400 CHN elemental analyzer. The magnetic stirring of the respective solutions of three different types of C-dots was carried out on IKA C-MAG HS 7 stirrer. AICIL muffle furnace used for thermal calcinations of the precursor sources.

The antibacterial activities of C-dots prepared from the upcycling of single use plastic waste i.e. plastic bags, plastic cups and plastic bottles was studied against Gram-positive and Gram-negative micro-organism. The agar well diffusion method and minimum zone of inhibition assay was used for the antibacterial studies (Dasa et al., 2019; Korkmaz et al., 2020). In brief, bacteria culture of E. coli, S. typhi, P. aeruginosa and S. aureus (Gram positive) bacteria was placed in nutrient broth. The bacterial culture in the presence of deionized water was taken as a positive control. Whereas, the effect of different concentrations i.e. 500, 1000 and 5000 ppm of P-CDs, C-CDs and B-CDs were used for the biocompatibility studies. The sterile conditions were maintained by autoclaving the agar solution for 2 h at 121 °C. Around 25 ml of Muller Hinton agar was utilized to fill the respective petri plates and allowed to incubate in laminar air flow at 37 °C for 24 h. An aliquot of pure strains of bacteria were placed in the respective petri plates by utilizing bacterial loop. Subsequently, the 5 mm diameter holes were made up in the plates with the help of puncher. The freshly synthesized 10 µl suspension of different concentrations of P-CDs, C-CDs and B-CDs were added into each well and incubated at 37 °C for 24 h. The reduction in the bacteria population was estimated out for the analysis.

The antifungal activity of the C-dots was analyzed against fungal strain of Laurilia taxodii. For the analysis, the fungal strain was freshly cultured in nutrient broth. Agar-Agar Type-Ι and Malt Extract were used for the grounding of petri plates. To sustain the sterile conditions, the Agar-Agar Type-Ι and Malt Extract solution was autoclaved for 2 h at 121 °C followed by cooling down to room temperature. Further on, the petri plates were incubated in laminar air flow chamber to solidify at 37 °C for 24 h (Suia et al., 2020; Miri et al., 2019). The three different concentrations i.e. 500 ppm, 1000 ppm, 5000 ppm of P-CDs, C-CDs and B-CDs were spread over the agar medium for investigating the antifungal activities. For each plate, an aliquot of same amount of the fungal species having a diameter of 3 mm was placed in the petri plates. The plate without C-dots was set as positive control. All the analysis was executed in triplicates. The nutrient agar plates were sealed properly and again incubated for 2 days at 37 °C (Pham et al., 2019). Eventually, the antifungal activity was evaluated by determining the zone of inhibition compared with respect to the control.

The substantial effect of different concentrations of synthesized C-dots was investigated at the genomic level by using the Allium cepa chromosomal aberration assay. For the experimentation, Allium cepa (Onion) of standard size and weight were purchased from local market of sector-14, Chandigarh, India. The onion bulbs were washed several times with deionized water after removing outer 2–3 layers with a sterilized knife. The extra root-tips were removed with the help of sterile scissor in order to allow the growth of fresh root-tips. Then, the processed onion bulbs were transferred to beakers containing different concentration of synthesized C-dots (500 and 1000 ppm) for 3 days. Onion bulbs dipped in deionized water were set up as a control experiment. After 3 days of incubation period, the newly emerged root-tips were collected, fixed using 1 N HCl, stained using methylene blue and studied under light microscope (NIKON ECLIPSE 450). The same experiment was repeated thrice for different concentrations of C-dots. The different stages of mitotic divisions (prophase, metaphase, anaphase, telophase and interphase) were counted and Mitotic index value was calculated using the equation (2) (Chaudhary et al., 2018, 2016; Fiskesjo, 1997).
(2)
Mitotic
Index ( MI ) = P + M + A + T / Total
number
of
cells
in
interphase
where P, M, A, T refers to prophase, metaphase, anaphase and telophase respectively.

With the aim of reducing the production cost, saving the natural landfills and reinforcing the environment by converting the plastic waste to useful and highly luminescent C-dots is the central theme of the work. The utilization of the single use plastic waste (i.e. poly bags, cups and bottles) has acted as the potential feeding stock for the fabrication of economically viable fluorescent C-dots. Thus, the current work presents the useful mean to upcycle low priced non-biodegradable plastic sources containing high value of carbon content to useful C-dots via simple thermal treatment at 400 °C. The successive purifications were done by ultracentrifugation at 7000 rpm. In comparison with the traditional hydrothermal heating methods for the fabrication of C-dots, the used methodology required comparatively low temperature and better control over the synthesis and yield of C-dots. Hence, the current methodology is more appropriate for scale-up production of C-dots. The outcomes of FTIR corresponded to the P-CDs, C-CDs and B-CDs are illustrated in Fig. 1 (a). For, P-CDs, the strong absorption peaks were observed at 2920, 2817 and 1341 cm−1 respectively. These peaks were aroused due to CH stretching and bending vibration in particles. The characteristic absorption peak around 3500 cm−1 has represented the existence of OH stretching mode in C-dots (Wu et al., 2020). The two peaks around 1629 and 716 cm−1 were owing due to the presence of CC bending vibration. The intense absorption peak at 1464 cm−1 was occurred due to the presence of COH groups over the surface of P-CDs. The small peak at 1070 cm−1 was due to the existence of CO and COC stretching (Yallappa et al., 2017). For C-CDs, the absorption peak around 3600 cm−1 was assigned to the stretching vibration of the OH functionalities of water molecule absorbed by the sample (Romero-cano et al., 2019). However the strong absorption peak at 3500 cm−1 was assigned to the stretching vibration of the –NH2 group (Romero-cano et al., 2019). The C-H bending vibrations were appeared at 1382 cm−1 for C-CDs. The peak at 992 cm−1 was due to the presence of the epoxy ring in the synthesized material. In case of B-CDs, the stretching vibration of –OH group was appeared around 3600 cm−1 (Gnanasambandam et al., 2000). The four peaks around 1710, 1392, 1238 and 1075 cm−1 has further supported the existence of CO stretching, CO stretching and COC group bending vibration in B-CDs. The small peak at 993 cm−1 was due to the presence of epoxy ring in B-CDs. The presence of functional group over the surface of formed C-dots has the ability to enhance the aqueous solubility and supported the applications in chemo-sensing (Scheme 1).

In the XRD profile (Fig. 1b), C-dots have exhibited the crystalline nature with various diffraction peaks. The major peaks for P-CDs were observed at 2θ = 21.5°, 23.9°, 29.5°, 30.9° and 36.2° correspond to the (100), (101), (102), (103) and (006) diffraction planes in P-CDs (JCPDS No. 06-0464) (Zhao et al., 2020). The peaks for C-CDs were observed at 2θ = 24.9°, 29.5°, 36.1°, 43.3° and 47.5° which ascribed to the (002), (102), (105), (200) and (110) planes in C-CDs (Quing et al., 2020). The peaks for B-CDs were observed at 2θ = 21.5°, 23.9°, 29.2°, 30.9° and 36.3° and ascribed to the (002), (101), (102), (103) and (006) plane (Mehta et al., 2014). Moreover, all the diffraction peaks observed in P-CDs, C-CDs and B-CDs specified the good crystalline nature of as synthesized C-dots. The crystalline sizes were studied by using the Debye-Scherrer equation (equation (3)) and found to be 22 nm, 30.4 nm and 9.1 nm for the P-CDs, C-CDs and B-CDs respectively.
(3)
D =
K λ
β Cos θ
where K corresponds to the dimensionless shape factor whose factor close to unity (0.94), λ corresponds to X-ray wavelength used, β corresponds to the full width at half maxima which is calculated from XRD spectrum and θ corresponds to Bragg’s angle which is also calculated from XRD spectrum.

The HRTEM images (Fig. 1 (c–e)) have further revealed the existence of high density of CDs in P-CDs, C-CDs and B-CDs respectively with average sizes range was 5–10 nm (Li et al., 2020; Linehan et al., 2014). The hydrodynamic diameter for P-CDs, C-CDs and B-CDs was found to be 27.9 nm, 28.3 nm and 7.5 nm respectively. The value of PDI was around 0.5, 0.4 and 0.3 for P-CDs, C-CDs and B-CDs respectively (Fig. S1 supplementary data). The obtained results have further confirmed the well dispersity and homogeneity of C-dots upcycled from plastic waste. From here, it has also been inferred that synthesized C-dots from plastic waste can be easily tuned within the confinement of nano range. EDX spectra give the elemental peaks of the desired carbon and oxygen elements in formed particles (Fig. S2 supplementary data). The absence of any kind of additional peaks has confirmed the formation of C-dots with high composition of carbon and oxygen and further reflects their purity. From the elemental CHNS analysis, it was also verified that the carbon (C) content was higher as compared to hydrogen (H) and nitrogen (N) for all the three synthesized samples (Table S2, supplementary data).

The thermal characteristic properties of the C-dots prepared from the upcycling of waste plastic were further analyzed by thermogravimetric (TGA) and differential scanning calorimetric analysis (DSC), as presented in Fig. 2 (a–b). In case of P-CDs, two major weight losses at between 150 °C and 650 °C were observed during the thermal decomposition of sample. The first weight loss was mainly associated with the loss of moisture absorbed over the surface of P-CDs. The second weight loss was associated with the pyrolysis of oxygen containing functional groups over the surface of P-CDs. In addition, the oxidative degradation of carbon skeleton in CDs were also responsible for major loss in P-CDs. In case of C-CDs, three major weight losses at 58.9 °C (58.9%), 202 °C (10.1%) and 560 °C (21.9%) were observed. The first weight loss was mainly ascribed to the vaporization of moisture content in the sample and breakage of loosely bounded water molecule on the surface of C-CDs. The second weight loss occurred were due to the abolition of different functional groups from the façade of C-CDs. The third weight loss was aroused due to pyrolysis of oxygen containing functional groups. Whereas for B-CDs, two major weight losses at between 150 °C and 650 °C were attributed due to the evaporation of water molecules and thermal decomposition of exterior functional moieties on the surface of surface of B-CDs. The results have indicated the high thermal stability and purity of all the three types of upcycled C-dots from plastic waste. The thermal decomposition changes in DSC analysis have displayed exothermic peaks in all the three types of C-dots (Fig. 2 b). The respective value of glass transition temperature was found to be 129.4 °C, 130 °C and 132 °C for P-CDs, C-CDs and B-CDs respectively. This experimental verification has additionally supported the high thermal stability of C-dots prepared via upcycling of plastic waste.

Fig. S3 has displayed a broad absorption band between 240 and 350 nm in the UV–vis. absorption spectra of P-CDs, C-CDs and B-CDs respectively. The corresponding absorption region has been mainly associated with the n-π* and π - π* transition of the CC and CO bonds over the surface of C-dots (Chaudhary et al., 2016). In addition, the formed particles have displayed the natural photoluminescence while irradiating the particles with ultraviolent (UV) light of 365 nm. This behavioral aspect was explained due to the existence of electronic transitions within the C-dots upcycled from plastic waste. The PL spectral changes have displayed excitation dependent emission behavior in all the three types of C-dots (Fig. 3 (a–c)). The emission intensity was found to be increase with increase in the excitation wavelength ranging from 260 nm to 310 nm with maximum PL peak between 420 and 425 nm. However, on further increasing the excitation wavelength i.e. 320 nm, the emission intensity was found to be decreased. These types of PL characteristic properties were mainly explained via the presence of different types of surface emissive traps in CDs obtained from the upcycled plastic waste. These changes in emission intensity were mainly associated with the photogenerated electrons and holes trapped at different surface sites of the produced CDs and related with the presence of their radiative recombination (Chen et al., 2014). Clearly, most of the excited electrons were trapped by multiple energy levels and returned to ground states which produce their as such fluorescence characteristics. The respective comparison between the excitation wavelength dependent fluorescence properties of P-CDs, C-CDs and B-CDs has also been verified by visualizing the maximum emission wavelength with respect to the excitation wavelength (Fig. S4 supplementary data). The outcome data has clearly revealed that all the three types of formed particles i.e. P-CDs, C-CDs and B-CDs have displayed slight red shift in the emission peak. The small difference between the emission maxima w.r.t excitation wavelength has clearly explained that formed excitons from the carbogenic cores were easily trapped at the surface defects over the exterior surface of C-dots before recombination. The contrasting behavior of formed C-dots has also been visualized by comparing the fluorescence quantum yield (Φ) with the previously reported data. The Φ values were found to be 62, 65 and 64% for P-CDs, C-CDs and B-CDs respectively at 310 nm excitation. The respective effect of excitation wavelength on the Φ values has also been studied to enhance the scope of prepared C-dots in fluorescence imaging applications. The multi-excitation wavelength behavior of Φ values for P-CDs, C-CDs and B-CDs has been calculated with excitation wavelength variation between 270 and 320 nm. The outcomes of the studies have provided the detailed overview of Φ values at multi excitation wavelengths. From Fig. 3 (d), it has been found that, the Φ values increased as the excitation wavelength increased from 280 to 310 nm. Out of all the three types of formed particles, C-CDs have displayed highest Φ value at 310 nm as compared to P-CDs and B-CDs. This behavioral variation was mainly explained due to the variation in the emission wavelength in all the three prepared samples. These variations were further associated with the surface defects and size of formed C-dots. The outcomes have further supported the potential aptitude of C-dots for chemosensing applications. The stability of the formed particles was checked by analyzing their emission behavior as well as by monitoring their visual aggregation tendency upto 30 days. The particular solutions for all three types of P-CDs, C-CDs and B-CDs have not shown any kind of visual aggregation in the samples and their emission behavior was found to be similar during the monitoring phase of 30 days. Hence, the formed particles generated from the upcycling of plastic waste possessed higher stability rate. The photolysis effect over P-CDs, C-CDs and B-CDs under the exposure of UV irradiation with wavelength 365 nm for 60 min has also confirmed their stability and potential aptitude in sensing. These properties made the prepared C-dots as a continual and persistent chemosensor for detecting metal ions in real water samples taken from different resources and strengthened its sensing aptitude.

The lower value of toxicity is one of the prime requisite conditions for the diverse range of potential applications in the environmental remediation. In the current work, the biocompatibility of P-CDs, C-CDs and B-CDs has been checked via studying the antibacterial and antifungal activities by employing zone of inhibition (Dasa et al., 2019) assay towards four types of bacterial strains: Gram positive (S. aureus) and Gram negative (E. Coli, P. aeruginosa and S. typhi) bacteria. Fig. 4 (a) represented the minimum zone of inhibition of individual samples of P-CDs, C-CDs and B-CDs in various concentrations.

As it can be clearly visualized from this figure, that the increment in the concentration ranging from 500 to 5000 ppm has not shown any antibacterial impact over the growth as compared to control regardless of C-dots concentration (Fig. 4 a). This behavioral aspect has been explained due to the increment in the electrolytic amount in presence of C-dots. The C-dots synthesized via using plastic waste have the tendency to reduce the induction of reactive oxygen species (ROS) due to surface passivity. The surface modification has further enhanced their binding ability with the micro-organisms. The declining amount of free radical species in bacterial cells has further supported their biocompatibility towards bacterial strains (Dadi et al., 2019). The ability of C-dots to affect the growth of chosen fungal species is one of the important studies to visualize the biocompatibility effect of different types of C-dots prepared from plastic waste. The mycelium growth rate method gives the detailed overview for the effect of P-CDs, C-CDs and B-CDs as a function of concentrations. The deionized water was utilized as a standard positive control system. From the data, it was found that 500 ppm amount of all the three P-CDs, C-CDs and B-CDs have exhibited maximal growth for Laurilia taxodii as compared to 1000 and 5000 ppm (Fig. 4 (b) and (c)) whereas least growth was observed in case of 5000 ppm. The growth of fungus was observed to be slightly decreased at higher concentrations (Fig. S5). This has been probably correlated to change in the growth behavior of Laurilia taxodii at higher concentrations of C-dots (Erturk, 2006).

Allium cepa (A. cepa) test has been considered in the current methodology for the assessment of toxicity induced by different concentrations (500 and 1000 ppm) of developed P-CDs (Fig. 5 ). On the other hand, the C-CDs and B-CDs results were shown in (Fig. S6 and Fig. S7) respectively. It has been considered as an easy and consistent test model to estimate various kinds of xenobiotic characteristics of C-dots in detail. Furthermore, the microscopic investigation has provided a direct visualization in the chromosomal aberrations exposed to the C-dots. Additionally, the genotypic damages and growth patterns of different stages of A. cepa has given clear evidence regarding the toxic information of the prepared C-dots (Chaudhary et al., 2018, 2016). From the data, it was found that the different concentrations of C-dots have exhibited the significant variation over the mitotic stages presented in the A. cepa root tip cells. The different chromosomal stages (interphase, prophase, metaphase, anaphase and telophase) were observed in each set of C-dots (Fig. 5). The value of mitotic index and relative mitotic index for P-CDs, C-CDs and B-CDs was found very much similar to the control system in every concentration of C-dots (Table S3, supplementary data). Additionally, the value of chromosomal aberration index was found negligible in C-dots which further suggested the biocompatible behavior of C-dots towards genetic levels. In case of 1000 ppm level, very few sticky chromosomes very observed due to slight excess concentration of C-dots. From the results, it has been verified that obtained values are exactly same at every concentration of C-dots as compared to the control system. The results have shown the higher rate of biocompatibility of developed C-dots. In this study, surface properties, good photo stability and high quantum yield of synthesized C-dots has influenced the enhanced activity of C-dots over the A. cepa. The absorption process of C-dots has prevented the formation of chemically reactive oxygen species (ROS) and produced the lower chromosomal aberration. In addition, the presence of C-dots has promoted the growth of different genetic levels of A. cepa along with DNA and does not create any chromosomal aberration. Hence, the formed C-dots (P-CDs, C-CDs and B-CDs) were found to be non-toxic in nature towards A. cepa and further promoted their growth.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical sensing performance of C-dots in presence of metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: The quenching in emission intensity was mainly attributed to the robust complex formation of copper ion with various functional groups (hydroxyl, carboxyl) at the façade of C-dots, which has further supported the chelation effect. The paramagnetic nature of copper ion has further supported the quenching of the fluorescence signal via electron transfer process (Wang et al., 2017). The sensitivity of the developed sensor was further studied by carrying out the quantitative detection of copper ion by using fluorescence-based titration method (Fig. 6 (d–e)). The concentration range of 0.02–50 µm of Cu2+ ions were chosen for the investigation. The fluorescence intensity has displayed a linear decrement on increasing the Cu2+ to each type of chosen C-dots. Herein, it can be concluded that produced C-dots have high efficiency for the sensitive detection of Cu2+ ions.

The quenching mechanism of Cu2+ ions in the presence of C-dots has further been verified by stern-Volmer relationship.
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K SV
[ Q ]
where F0 and F correspond to the intensity of C-dots in the absence and presence of copper ion, KSV refers to the stern-Volmer association constant and Q is the copper concentration in the solution. The limit of detection (LOD) was determined using standard formula below:
LOD = 3 S . D . / P

Herein, S.D. refers to the standard deviation and P refers to the slope of the of stern-Volmer plot. The Benesi-Hildebrand plot was also utilized to calculate the binding efficiency of C-dots with Cu2+ metal ion (Fig. S7). The detection limit of developed sensor was found to be 0.24 µm, 0.15 µm and 0.43 µm for P-CDs, C-CDs and B-CDs respectively. The detection limit is comparable with or better than reported results in the literature for sensing of Cu2+ ions (Table S4, supplementary data). The corresponding values of detection limit (LOD), quantitation limit, fluorescence quenching factor and binding constant have been mentioned in Table S5.

The obtained results have clearly revealed that the fabricated C-dots can provide efficient sensor probe for selective and sensitive detection of copper ion up to a lower limit of detection in water sources by fluorescence quenching method. The reliability of designed sensor was checked by performing all the experiments in the set of triplicates. In order to check whether the proposed sensor is selective for Cu2+ sensing, 13 other common cations were also investigated. Particularly, these cations including Ni2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Fe2+, K+, Hg2+, Na+, Pb2+, Ca2+, Co2+, Mg2+, Ba2+ were checked for the same conditions in the presence of 50 µm concentration of Cu2+. By comparing fluorescence intensities of Cu2+ with those of the other targets metal ions in Fig. 7 (a–c), it has been clearly seen that only Cu2+ provoked the quenching aptitude for all three types of P-CDs, C-CDs and B-CDs, whereas other chosen cations have demonstrated small quenching effect, which convincingly recommends that the as-prepared C-dots were quite selective for Cu2+ over the other related cations. Especially, in case of B-CDs, the emission intensity of C-dots and Cu2+ does not changes much in presence of various other metal ions which suggest the ultimate selectivity of C-dots towards Cu2+ ion. The occurrence of strong association between Cu2+ with hydroxyl and carbonyl groups over the surface has led to formation of non-radiative recombination of charges and produced highest selectivity for Cu2+. These outcomes have further confirmed that the C-dots prepared through the upcycling of plastic wastes are quite sensitive and displayed exceptional high selectivity for Cu2+ sensing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical sensing performance of C-dots in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: The practical viability of the developed sensor in the area of metal ion sensing has further been checked in presence of real water samples taken from different sources. To study the effect on the developed chemo-sensor probe, three real water samples (lake water, tap water and well water) were collected from the local sites of Chandigarh and spiked with different concentration of copper ion (Fig. 8 (a–c)). The fluorescence intensity of the synthesized C-dots in the presence of real water samples having different concentration of copper ion has displayed significant quenching (turn off) under laboratory conditions. These outcomes have further confirmed that the C-dots prepared through the upcycling of plastic wastes are quite sensitive and displayed exceptional high selectivity for Cu2+ sensing in real water samples. The average recovery of as-prepared samples was observed 92–98%.

To summarize, the facile synthesis of three different types of C-dots have been presented by using upcycled plastic wastes. The current strategy has offered a simple way for the formation of useful advanced nanomaterials by dipping the cost of required raw materials. The upcycling of plastic waste to useful nanomaterial is one of the captivating issues in scientific world due to the profitable impact on ecological system. The synthesis process was carefully optimized for scalable mass production of C-dots with better control over the size and optical properties. The fabricated C-dots have possessed different types of functional groups like COOH and OH over the exterior surface and presenting admirable water solubility. The biocompatibility of developed C-dots was verified by using multi-assay approach including fungus, bacteria and at the chromosomal level which further enhance their potential use in environmental remediation activities. Another outcome of this work is that C-dots have provided the excitation dependent variation in the quantum behavior which will further useful for practical applications, mainly given the excitation of cellular auto fluorescence. The prepared C-dots have also proved to be outstanding nanosensors for the fluorescence quenching recognition of one of the main heavy metal ion pollutants of ecological concern i.e. Cu2+ ion. With a large framework, the outcomes of the current studies have highlighted the potential accomplishment in preserving environment fate and giving response towards the budding social hitch of plastic waste.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Savita Chaudhary is thankful to DST Inspire Faculty award [IFACH- 17], Haryana State Council for Science and Technology [HSCSIT/R&D/2020/476] and DST Purse grants II for financial assistance. Authors are thankful to Panjab University SAIF CIL and Department of Environment Studies Panjab University Chandigarh for technical support.
